meta.title: The Promise That Binds Tomorrow
meta.theme: why a promise creates an obligation that mere prediction does not

format.format_verified: 2026-07: five sentences, TITA, +3 with no negative marking, consistent in released papers 2016 to 2025; re-verify against each new official paper

traps.description: Trap A: a genuinely belonging core sentence (E) is dressed oddly, opening with a bare and and shifting to the second person, to lure a shortcut reader into excluding it instead of the true definitional intruder.

meta.learning_objective: resist excluding a belonging sentence for a surface oddity while a general definition hides in plain sight

source.usage_note: Original composition in CAT register for CAT OS. General moral philosophy of promising; no specific text adapted. Personal study only.

meta.prompt_version: in-repo authored (Odd One Out milestone); ODD_MAN_OUT_BIBLE frameworks applied
meta.reviewer_notes: Advanced, first Trap A. The core argues why a promise binds where prediction does not; the outlier A is a broad general definition of obligation. Meanwhile the belonging sentence E is dressed with a bare and and a second person shift to draw the eye. Outlier at A. Owner review pending.

sentences.label: A
sentences.text: An obligation, in the broadest sense, is any consideration that counts in favour of acting one way rather than another.

sentences.label: B
sentences.text: If I merely predict that I will help you move house, I have told you something about the future that may simply turn out false.

sentences.label: C
sentences.text: But if I promise to help, I have done something stranger: I have reached into tomorrow and placed a claim there that you may now hold against me.

sentences.label: D
sentences.text: The prediction leaves me free to change; the promise does not, for breaking it is not being mistaken but wronging the person I bound myself to.

sentences.label: E
sentences.text: And so the words, once said, are no longer only yours, for you have handed the other person a small piece of your own future to keep.

builder.prompt: Build the four sentence argument for how a promise differs from a prediction, from the contrast to its point. The sentence left out is your answer.

explanation.spine_note: The four sentences argue one contrast. B gives the prediction, a claim about the future that can simply be false. C gives the promise, which places a claim in tomorrow that another may hold. D sharpens the difference, breaking a promise is wronging someone, not being mistaken. E draws the point, the words become partly the other person's. Prediction, promise, difference, point.

core_roles.label: B
core_roles.why_here: It opens the contrast with the first term, a prediction, which every later sentence measures the promise against.

core_roles.label: C
core_roles.why_here: It introduces the second term with but if I promise, setting the promise directly against B's prediction.

core_roles.label: D
core_roles.why_here: It states the crux of the contrast, that breaking a promise wrongs a person where a false prediction merely errs.

core_roles.label: E
core_roles.why_here: It draws the argument to its point, that a promise hands part of your future to another to keep.

links.explanation: C opens with but if I promise, explicitly turning against the prediction B set up, the contrast the argument runs on.
D's the prediction and the promise stand in for the two cases B and C described, weighing them against each other.
E opens with and so, drawing D's difference to the conclusion, the promised words becoming partly the other's.

explanation.violation_named: Sentence A is a general definition inside a specific argument. The core argues one focused contrast, the promise against the prediction, in the first person, with a claim, a distinction and a point. A defines obligation in the broadest sense, a textbook generalisation that stands above the particular contrast the four sentences are making.
explanation.why_it_breaks: The argument never needs a definition of obligation in general; it needs the difference between two specific speech acts. A's broad definition connects to nothing: no core sentence takes up the abstract notion of a consideration counting in favour of acting. It could open a general essay on ethics. This paragraph is a tight case about promising, and a definition pitched at the whole field has no join within it.
explanation.trap_exposed: Two things are happening. The true intruder A is disguised as a dignified opening line, the kind of definition a philosophy paragraph often begins with, so its generality reads as a frame rather than a fault. Meanwhile the belonging sentence E is dressed to look odd, opening with a bare and so and switching to you, giving a shortcut reader a surface reason to cut it. Resist both. E carries the argument's point and joins D by substitution; A defines a whole field and joins nothing.

exclusion_analysis.label: B
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: Its everyday example of helping someone move can look too casual to belong beside the abstract sentences.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without B the contrast has only one term: C's but if I promise turns against a prediction the paragraph never made.

exclusion_analysis.label: C
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: Its dense image of reaching into tomorrow is the most abstract sentence, and its exact place is hard to fix before building.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without C the promise itself is missing, and D contrasts the prediction with a claim the paragraph never introduced.

exclusion_analysis.label: D
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: D and A both make general statements about obligation, so a hurried read may hold both as candidates and guess.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without D the sharp difference between wronging and erring is gone, and E's conclusion rests on a distinction never drawn.

exclusion_analysis.label: E
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: This is the planted decoy: its bare opening and its switch to you make it feel tonally out of step with the rest.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without E the argument states its difference but never lands its point, that a promise gives part of your future away.

explanation.detection_habit: A surface oddity is a costume, not a verdict, and a dignified general definition is often the real intruder. Build the argument first, then keep the sentence that carries its point and cut the one that defines the whole field.

mentor.challenge: One sentence here dresses oddly but does real work. Another dresses respectably and does none. Judge by the work, not the dress.
mentor.takeaway: The odd looking sentence often belongs, and the textbook definition often does not. Structure hides under style.
